1: māh-tithalēl bĕrāʿāh haggîbbôr — why do you boast of evil, O powerful man? The ironic address: gibbôr (mighty man, warrior, hero) — the title of honor applied with bitter irony to Doeg the Edomite, whose boast is in betrayal rather than valor. ḥesed ʾēl kol-hayyôm — the steadfast love of God holds all the day. The theological counter to the hero's boasting: while the gibbôr boasts in wickedness, the divine ḥesed endures through all the day. The juxtaposition of the wicked hero's momentary triumph against the permanent divine steadfast love.
2: hawwôt taḥshōb lĕshônekā — your tongue plots ruin. Ḥāshab (to devise, think out, plan — the deliberate, calculated planning of harm; the tongue as the instrument of the planning). Hawwāh (ruin, destruction — from hāwāh: to fall; the catastrophic ruin brought by the wicked tongue). kĕtaʿar mĕlûṭāsh — sharp as a razor blade. Taʿar mĕlûṭāsh (a sharpened razor/knife) — the tongue as a cutting weapon. The tongue-as-weapon metaphor (cf. Ps 57:4; 64:3) applied to Doeg whose words to Saul caused the massacre of the Nob priests (1 Sam 22:9-10, 18-19). ʿōśēh rĕmiyyāh — you craftsman of deceit. Rĕmiyyāh (deceit, treachery — the deceptive quality that makes the razor-tongue doubly dangerous).
3: ʾāhabtā rāʿ miṭṭôb — you love evil rather than good. The choice is not ignorance but love (ʾāhab: to love — the affective, volitional commitment to evil over good). sheqer middabbēr ṣedeq selāh — and lies rather than truth. Sheqer (lie, falsehood — the spoken word that diverges from reality) loved more than ṣedeq (righteousness, truth, right-speaking). The selah marks the weight of the verdict before the consequence is described.
4: ʾāhabtā khol-dibĕrê-baladaʿ — you love every word that destroys. Baladaʿ (swallowing, destruction — from bālaʿ: to swallow up). Every destructive word is loved — the comprehensive appetite for ruinous speech. lĕshôn mirmāh — you treacherous tongue. The direct address to the tongue itself — the tongue named for its defining quality: mirmāh (deceit, treachery — the betrayal that the tongue performs).
5: gam-ʾēl yiṭṭāḥăkhā lāneṣaḥ — but God will also pull you down permanently. Nātaḥ (hiphil: to demolish, throw down — the tearing down of what has been built up). Lāneṣaḥ (permanently, forever — in contrast to the temporary success of the wicked). yaḥtĕkhā wĕyissāḥăkhā meʾōhel — he will grab and rip you from your home. Ḥātāh (to seize — as one seizes burning coals with tongs) + nāsaḥ (to tear out, uproot — as a plant is torn from the soil). wĕshēreshshĕkhā mēʾereṣ ḥayyîm selāh — he will tear you out of the land of the living. Shāresh (piel: to root out, to remove completely by the roots — the destruction that reaches to the foundational level). ʾEreṣ ḥayyîm (the land of the living — the world of the living, in contrast to Sheol). The selah marks the divine verdict before the community's response.
6: wĕyirʾû ṣaddîqîm wĕyîrāʾû — the righteous will see and be filled with awe. The communal response to the wicked one's downfall: yirʾû (they will see — witness the event) + yîrāʾû (they will fear/be awed — the reverential response to divine judgment). wĕʿālāyw yiśḥāqû — and they will laugh. Śāḥaq (to laugh — not cruel mockery but the laughter of vindication; the laughter of those who were threatened by the powerful wicked person and now see the divine reversal).
7: hinnēh haggeber lōʾ yāśîm ʾĕlōhîm maʿûzzô — here is the man who would not take refuge in God. Śîm maʿôz (to set/make as a fortress — to adopt as one's stronghold). The man who refused to make God his stronghold. wayyibṭaḥ bĕrōb ʿoshĕrô — but relied on his great wealth. Bāṭaḥ bĕ (to trust in — the same verb as Ps 49:6 where the foolish trust in wealth). yaʿōz bĕhawwātô — and found his strength in destruction. ʿāzaz bĕhawwāh (to make oneself strong through ruin — the power built on the destruction of others: Doeg's strength was built on the ruin of Ahimelech and the Nob priests).
8: waʾănî kĕzayit raʿănān bĕbêt ʾĕlōhîm — but I am like a flourishing olive tree in the house of God. Zayit raʿănān (a flourishing/green olive tree — raʿănān: fresh, lush, full of life as in Ps 92:14). The contrast with the uprooted wicked (v5): where Doeg is torn from his home, David is like an olive tree planted in the divine house. The olive tree's longevity (centuries), its productivity (oil for light, food, and anointing), and its evergreen character make it a rich image for enduring covenant life. bāṭaḥtî bĕḥesed-ʾĕlōhîm ʿôlām wāʿed — I trust in God's steadfast love forever and ever. The ground of the olive-tree flourishing: trust (bāṭaḥ) in the divine ḥesed — the opposite of the wicked's trust in wealth and destructive power.
9: ʾôdekhā lĕʿôlām kî ʿāśîtā — I will give you thanks forever, because of what you have done. Yādāh (to give thanks, to acknowledge publicly) — the thanksgiving that Ps 50 identified as the true sacrifice (tôdāh). wăʾăqawweh shimĕkhā kî-ṭôb neged ḥăsîdêkhā — I will hope in your name, for it is good, before your faithful people. Qāwāh (to hope, wait for — the patient expectation oriented toward the divine name). The final word: in the presence of the ḥăsîdîm (the faithful ones — those who embody the ḥesed that is the opposite of Doeg's treachery) the Psalmist will declare the goodness of the divine name.
